questions.questionText: Measles is characterized by the presence of pathognomonic:
questions.optionB: Koplik spots (bluish-white spots on buccal mucosa opposite lower molars — appear 2 days before rash, pathognomonic)
questions.explanation: Measles (rubeola): paramyxovirus, highly contagious. Prodrome: high fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis (3 C's). Koplik spots: pathognomonic — tiny bluish-white spots on erythematous base on buccal mucosa (opposite 2nd molars), appear 1-2 days before rash. Rash: maculopapular, starts at face/behind ears → spreads downward (craniocaudal), lasts 5-6 days, clears in same order. Complications: pneumonia (most common cause of death), otitis media, encephalitis, SSPE (subacute sclerosing panencephalitis — late, years later). Reference: OP Ghai — Measles.

questions.questionText: Kawasaki disease diagnostic criteria require fever ≥5 days plus at least 4 of 5 features:
questions.optionB: Bilateral conjunctival injection, oral mucosal changes (strawberry tongue/cracked lips), extremity changes (edema/desquamation), polymorphous rash, and cervical lymphadenopathy (≥1.5 cm)
questions.explanation: Kawasaki disease: acute febrile vasculitis of childhood (peak 1-5 years, Japan/Asian descent). Diagnosis: fever ≥5 days + ≥4 of 5 criteria (CRASH): Conjunctival injection (bilateral, non-purulent), Rash (polymorphous), Adenopathy (cervical, ≥1.5 cm, usually unilateral), Strawberry tongue/oral changes (cracked lips, pharyngeal erythema), Hand/feet changes (edema → periungual desquamation in convalescence). MOST FEARED complication: coronary artery aneurysm (15-25% untreated). Treatment: IVIG (2 g/kg single dose) + high-dose aspirin → low-dose aspirin. Reference: OP Ghai — Kawasaki.

questions.questionText: The most common cause of acute diarrhea in children worldwide is:
questions.optionB: Rotavirus (most common cause of severe dehydrating diarrhea in children <5 years — oral rotavirus vaccine in NIS)
questions.explanation: Rotavirus: most common viral cause of severe gastroenteritis/diarrhea in children <5 years worldwide. Responsible for 30-40% of diarrheal hospitalizations. Features: watery diarrhea (non-bloody), vomiting, fever, dehydration. Peak: 6 months - 2 years. Transmission: fecal-oral. Season: winter in temperate, year-round in tropical. Vaccine: oral live attenuated (Rotavac in India NIS — 3 doses at 6, 10, 14 weeks). ORS (oral rehydration solution): mainstay of treatment. Zinc supplementation: 10-14 days (reduces duration/recurrence). Reference: OP Ghai — Rotavirus.
